content.text: Inference engines are specialized software runtimes that take a trained LLM and execute forward passes as efficiently as possible. Where a training framework like PyTorch is optimized for gradient computation and batching across a research cluster, an inference engine strips away everything that is not needed for generation and replaces it with techniques — PagedAttention, continuous batching, quantized kernels, hardware-specific memory layouts — that maximize tokens per second and minimize time to first token. The two most widely used open-source inference engines are
, which targets high-throughput GPU serving, and
, which targets portability and CPU-class or consumer GPU hardware.

An inference engine is the software layer that sits between a stored model checkpoint and the application that needs a completion. It handles weight loading, tokenization, KV cache management, request scheduling, and dispatching compute to hardware backends. Two engines dominate open-source local inference:
a Python-based inference server built on PyTorch, designed to serve many concurrent users at high throughput on NVIDIA and AMD GPUs using PagedAttention and continuous batching.
a pure C and C++ implementation of LLaMA-family inference with no CUDA or Python dependency, portable to CPUs, Apple Silicon, and edge devices via the GGUF weight format.
both engines expose an OpenAI-compatible REST API at /v1/chat/completions, making them drop-in replacements for client code written against the OpenAI SDK.
the key-value tensor cache that grows with context length is the central resource both engines must manage efficiently. It determines how many concurrent sequences can be in flight.
vLLM dispatches to CUDA or ROCm kernels; llama.cpp dispatches to CUDA, Metal, Vulkan, or plain AVX2/AVX-512 SIMD depending on what is available at compile time.

A naively implemented inference loop processes one request at a time. Between each request, the GPU sits idle while the next prompt is tokenized and the result is returned to the caller. On a 40-series NVIDIA GPU capable of hundreds of teraFLOPS, this idle time means utilization well under ten percent. Inference engines close that gap. They keep the GPU busy across the full duration of many simultaneous conversations, and they manage memory so that the KV cache for longer contexts does not fragment GPU VRAM into unusable gaps that leave the accelerator underutilized even when requests are pending.

For local development and deployment, the choice of inference engine also determines which models you can run at all. A 70-billion-parameter model in bfloat16 requires 140 GB of VRAM — far beyond any single consumer GPU. But in a 4-bit quantization loaded by llama.cpp, the same model fits in about 40 GB and can run across a 64 GB Apple M2 Ultra's unified memory. The inference engine is the layer that makes large models accessible to hardware that was never designed for them.
continuous batching in vLLM keeps the GPU busy across hundreds of simultaneous requests.
PagedAttention allocates KV cache in small fixed pages, like virtual memory, instead of requiring contiguous regions per sequence.
both engines expose a /v1/chat/completions endpoint, making them drop-in replacements for OpenAI SDK client code.
llama.cpp supports CUDA, Metal, Vulkan, and pure CPU paths, so the same code runs on a MacBook Air, a Linux workstation, and a Raspberry Pi.
GGUF (llama.cpp) and AWQ/GPTQ (vLLM) formats reduce model memory by 4–8x with manageable quality loss.
vLLM can split model layers across multiple GPUs transparently, enabling models larger than any single GPU's VRAM.
vLLM supports using a small draft model to propose multiple tokens at once, which the large model verifies in a single forward pass, reducing latency.
vLLM requires NVIDIA or AMD GPUs:
it does not run on CPU or Apple Silicon in production mode, limiting its use to GPU-equipped servers.
loading a large model can take 30 to 90 seconds before the server is ready due to the Python and PyTorch initialization stack.
on CPU, even a quantized 7B model may generate only 5 to 15 tokens per second, compared to 100+ on a modern GPU.
aggressive 2-bit or 3-bit quantizations can noticeably degrade instruction-following and tool-use accuracy.
when many requests are in flight simultaneously, individual request latency increases even if aggregate throughput is high.
each engine supports a curated list of architectures. A newly released model architecture may not be available until maintainers merge the required attention kernel changes.

Traditional attention implementations allocate one contiguous block of GPU memory per sequence to hold the KV cache. When requests arrive and depart at different rates — as they do in production — these contiguous blocks cannot be reused until a sequence is complete. Gaps accumulate in VRAM. vLLM's PagedAttention, modeled on OS virtual memory, divides the KV cache pool into fixed-size pages (typically 16 or 32 tokens each). A block table maps each sequence's logical KV positions to physical pages scattered anywhere in VRAM. When a sequence finishes, its pages return immediately to the free pool and are available to new sequences. This eliminates fragmentation and allows the GPU to sustain a much larger number of concurrent sequences with the same VRAM budget.

Continuous batching adds the second major efficiency gain. Rather than waiting for all sequences in a batch to finish before starting new ones, vLLM's scheduler adds newly arriving requests to the currently running batch at each decoding step. A request that completes mid-batch does not leave the GPU idle — its slot is immediately assigned to the next pending request. This raises effective GPU utilization from the 20–40% typical of static batching to 60–90% under moderate load.

llama.cpp reimplements the entire LLaMA forward pass in C and C++, with no dependency on PyTorch or CUDA for CPU inference. The GGUF file format stores model weights, tokenizer vocabulary, and quantization metadata in a single file. The quantization schemes — Q4_K_M, Q5_K_S, Q8_0, and others — group weights into small blocks and represent each block with a shared scale factor plus low-bit integers. This allows the SIMD units in a modern CPU to process multiple weight values in parallel using integer instructions, achieving meaningful throughput without a GPU.

When a CUDA, Metal, or Vulkan backend is enabled at compile time, llama.cpp offloads compute-bound attention and feed-forward layers to the accelerator, while keeping overflow layers on CPU RAM. The number of layers offloaded to GPU is controlled by the --n-gpu-layers flag, allowing partial acceleration of models too large to fit entirely in VRAM.
The diagram below shows the request flow through an inference engine, from the HTTP client through scheduling and memory management to the hardware backend.

attrs.alt: Inference Engine Architecture showing request flow through API Server, Scheduler with continuous batching, Worker with PagedAttention KV cache, and GPU/CPU backends for vLLM and llama.cpp

content.text: You have NVIDIA or AMD GPUs with at least 16 GB of VRAM available.
You need to serve many concurrent users simultaneously and throughput is the primary metric.
You need speculative decoding to reduce per-token latency for a specific model family.
You are running a production API endpoint and want a hardened request queue and OpenAI-compatible server.
You need multi-GPU tensor parallelism to run models larger than a single GPU's VRAM.
You are running on a laptop, Mac, or edge device without a data-center GPU.
You want zero Python or CUDA dependencies — a single compiled binary and a GGUF file is all that is needed.
You need to run a model partially on CPU due to VRAM constraints, using layer offloading.
You are embedding inference into a larger C or C++ application, mobile app, or embedded system.
You need the broadest hardware coverage: one codebase runs on x86, ARM, POWER, and WebAssembly.

# Install vLLM (Python 3.10+, requires CUDA-capable GPU)
pip install vllm

# Start an OpenAI-compatible API server
python -m vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server   --model meta-llama/Llama-3.2-8B-Instruct   --tensor-parallel-size 1   --max-model-len 8192   --host 0.0.0.0   --port 8000

# For multi-GPU tensor parallelism across 2 GPUs:
python -m vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server   --model meta-llama/Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct   --tensor-parallel-size 2   --max-model-len 32768
Querying vLLM with the OpenAI Python SDK

from openai import OpenAI

# Point the OpenAI client at the local vLLM server
client = OpenAI(
    base_url="http://localhost:8000/v1",
    api_key="not-required",  # vLLM does not enforce auth by default
)

response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="meta-llama/Llama-3.2-8B-Instruct",
    messages=[
        {"role": "system", "content": "You are a helpful assistant."},
        {"role": "user", "content": "Explain PagedAttention in two sentences."},
    ],
    temperature=0.2,
    max_tokens=256,
)
print(response.choices[0].message.content)

# Streaming response
stream = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="meta-llama/Llama-3.2-8B-Instruct",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "List the planets."}],
    stream=True,
)
for chunk in stream:
    if chunk.choices[0].delta.content:
        print(chunk.choices[0].delta.content, end="", flush=True)

# Build llama.cpp with CUDA support
git clone https://github.com/ggerganov/llama.cpp
cd llama.cpp
cmake -B build -DGGML_CUDA=ON    # Use -DGGML_METAL=ON for macOS
cmake --build build --config Release -j4

# Download a GGUF model (Llama 3.2 3B Q4 quantization, approx 2 GB)
# Models are available on HuggingFace from bartowski, TheBloke, lmstudio-community
wget https://huggingface.co/bartowski/Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct-GGUF/resolve/main/Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct-Q4_K_M.gguf

# Start the OpenAI-compatible HTTP server
./build/bin/llama-server   --model Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct-Q4_K_M.gguf   --n-gpu-layers 99   --ctx-size 8192   --host 0.0.0.0   --port 8080

# Test with curl
curl http://localhost:8080/v1/chat/completions   -H "Content-Type: application/json"   -d '{"model":"llama","messages":[{"role":"user","content":"Hello!"}]}'

# Benchmark throughput with the built-in bench tool
./build/bin/llama-bench   -m Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct-Q4_K_M.gguf   -p 512 -n 128 -ngl 99
Unified Python Client for Both Engines
Because both vLLM and llama.cpp expose an OpenAI-compatible API, a single client helper works with either engine by changing only the base_url parameter.

import time
from openai import OpenAI, APIConnectionError

def make_client(base_url: str, retries: int = 3) -> OpenAI:
    """Create an OpenAI-compatible client with retry on connection failure."""
    client = OpenAI(base_url=base_url, api_key="not-required")
    for attempt in range(retries):
        try:
            client.models.list()
            return client
        except APIConnectionError:
            if attempt == retries - 1:
                raise
            time.sleep(2 ** attempt)
    return client

vllm_client  = make_client("http://localhost:8000/v1")
llamacpp_client = make_client("http://localhost:8080/v1")

def complete(client: OpenAI, model: str, prompt: str) -> str:
    """Single-turn completion, streamed and collected into a string."""
    chunks = []
    stream = client.chat.completions.create(
        model=model,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
        stream=True,
        temperature=0.1,
        max_tokens=512,
    )
    for chunk in stream:
        delta = chunk.choices[0].delta.content
        if delta:
            chunks.append(delta)
    return "".join(chunks)

# Both engines expose the same interface
vllm_answer = complete(
    vllm_client,
    "meta-llama/Llama-3.2-8B-Instruct",
    "Summarize PagedAttention in one sentence.",
)
llamacpp_answer = complete(
    llamacpp_client,
    "llama",
    "Summarize GGUF quantization in one sentence.",
)

print("vLLM answer:", vllm_answer)
print("llama.cpp answer:", llamacpp_answer)

The following script starts a llama.cpp server for each quantization variant in sequence and records tokens per second, giving a direct comparison of speed versus model quality trade-offs on the same hardware.

#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""Compare generation latency across three quantization levels of the same model.
Assumes llama-server is on PATH and the three GGUF files are in the current directory.
"""
import subprocess
import time
import requests
import json

QUANTS = [
    ("Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct-Q4_K_M.gguf", "Q4_K_M"),
    ("Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct-Q8_0.gguf",   "Q8_0"),
]

PROMPT = "Describe the transformer architecture in detail, covering attention, feed-forward, and positional encoding."
PORT   = 8090

for model_file, label in QUANTS:
    proc = subprocess.Popen(
        ["llama-server", "--model", model_file, "--n-gpu-layers", "99",
         "--port", str(PORT), "--host", "127.0.0.1"],
        stdout=subprocess.DEVNULL,
        stderr=subprocess.DEVNULL,
    )
    # Wait for the server to be ready
    for _ in range(30):
        try:
            requests.get(f"http://127.0.0.1:{PORT}/health", timeout=1)
            break
        except Exception:
            time.sleep(1)

    t0 = time.perf_counter()
    resp = requests.post(
        f"http://127.0.0.1:{PORT}/v1/chat/completions",
        json={
            "model": "llama",
            "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": PROMPT}],
            "max_tokens": 256,
            "temperature": 0.0,
        },
        timeout=120,
    )
    elapsed = time.perf_counter() - t0
    data    = resp.json()
    tokens  = data["usage"]["completion_tokens"]
    tok_per_sec = tokens / elapsed

    print(f"{label}: {tokens} tokens in {elapsed:.1f}s = {tok_per_sec:.1f} tok/s")
    proc.terminate()
    proc.wait()
    time.sleep(2)

A startup building a document review assistant needed to process hundreds of uploaded contracts simultaneously in a private on-premises deployment. They evaluated both engines on a 4x A100 80 GB server running a 70B instruct model. With static batching and a naive inference loop, throughput peaked at 150 tokens per second across all concurrent users. Switching to vLLM with tensor-parallel-size 4 and PagedAttention, throughput reached 1,800 tokens per second with the same hardware — a 12x improvement — because the GPU stayed near 90% utilization rather than idling between requests. The OpenAI-compatible endpoint meant their existing Python client code needed no changes beyond the base_url parameter.

A separate team at the same company needed a lightweight assistant embedded in a desktop application shipped to lawyers' laptops. They used llama.cpp compiled for Windows and macOS with AVX2 and Metal acceleration respectively. A Llama 3.2 3B Q4_K_M model fit in under 3 GB of RAM, required no internet connection, and generated responses at 18–35 tokens per second on typical 2023-era laptops. The two deployments — vLLM in the data center and llama.cpp on the desktop — both spoke the same OpenAI-compatible API, so the application layer was identical.
Using a context length larger than the model was trained on:
setting --max-model-len to 128K on a model trained with an 8K context window causes incoherent outputs beyond the training window and wastes memory on an oversized KV cache.
without this flag, llama.cpp defaults to CPU-only inference even when a capable GPU is present. Setting --n-gpu-layers 99 offloads all layers to GPU on most models.
Running vLLM without sufficient VRAM for the KV cache:

vLLM pre-allocates a large fraction of available VRAM for the KV cache pool at startup. If the model weights leave too little room, vLLM will crash or refuse to start. Reduce --max-model-len to shrink the KV cache footprint.
Loading FP16 weights when 4-bit quantization would suffice:

for development and experimentation, the quality difference between FP16 and Q4_K_M is small, but the memory difference is 4x. Always quantize unless you are specifically evaluating quality degradation.
Assuming tensor parallelism improves latency linearly:
splitting across more GPUs adds inter-GPU communication overhead. For a single-user latency-sensitive use case, one GPU with the right model size often beats four GPUs with a much larger model.
Match the engine to the deployment target:
vLLM for GPU servers with multiple concurrent users; llama.cpp for laptops, edge devices, or deployments with no CUDA dependency.
Pin model and engine versions together:
a new vLLM release may change default behaviors or PagedAttention parameters. Test your workload after upgrades.
the default context length is the model's full training context, which maximizes KV cache memory use. Constrain it to your application's actual needs.
for interactive applications, streaming the response token-by-token as it is generated reduces perceived latency significantly compared to waiting for the full completion.
Validate quantized model accuracy on your task:
run a small eval suite comparing FP16 and your target quantization on the specific instructions or tool-call formats your application uses before deploying the quantized version.
even with PagedAttention, watch vLLM's block utilization. Extremely long sequences consume large numbers of pages and leave less room for other requests under peak load.

vLLM's PagedAttention and continuous batching allow a single A100 80 GB GPU to sustain 2,000–4,000 tokens per second on a 7B model under load — roughly 10–20x the throughput of a naively batched server. The KV cache pool size, controlled implicitly by --max-model-len and --gpu-memory-utilization, is the primary tunable parameter. Increasing gpu-memory-utilization from the default 0.90 to 0.95 allows more concurrent sequences but leaves less headroom for CUDA kernel allocations, which can cause out-of-memory errors under burst load.

llama.cpp throughput on CPU is dominated by memory bandwidth. A modern desktop CPU (Ryzen 9 7950X) with 128 GB of DDR5 at 96 GB/s memory bandwidth can generate roughly 8–12 tokens per second on a Q4 7B model. Apple M-series chips, with their unified memory architecture providing 200–400 GB/s bandwidth to both CPU and GPU, achieve 40–80 tokens per second on the same model using the Metal backend — a meaningful speed advantage over discrete GPU setups for models that fit in unified memory.
integrates with LangChain, LlamaIndex, Haystack, and any OpenAI-compatible client library. The vLLM project releases Docker images on DockerHub for rapid server deployment.
the llama-cpp-python package wraps the C library and exposes both a low-level Python API and an OpenAI-compatible FastAPI server.
the primary source for GGUF model files, with contributors like bartowski and TheBloke providing pre-quantized variants of most popular open models.
a higher-level tool that wraps llama.cpp with a model library, CLI, and managed daemon — covered in detail in the next sub-subtopic.
EleutherAI's benchmark suite, compatible with both vLLM and llama.cpp, for measuring accuracy degradation after quantization.
TVM compilation to WebGPU, Metal, CUDA

TensorRT-LLM, from NVIDIA, compiles a model graph to optimized CUDA kernels ahead of inference time, achieving the highest raw throughput on NVIDIA hardware but at the cost of a long compilation step and NVIDIA-only portability. Text Generation Inference (TGI) from Hugging Face is the closest competitor to vLLM, with a Rust API layer and similar PagedAttention support, often used when tight Hugging Face Hub integration is important.
both vLLM and llama.cpp's server modes listen without authentication. Always place an API gateway or reverse proxy with auth in front of any inference server exposed beyond localhost.
download GGUF files only from trusted sources and verify checksums before loading. A malicious GGUF file could exploit the weight loader.

on a multi-tenant server, vLLM's KV cache pool is shared across users. Sequence content is not leaked between users, but a user generating very long sequences can exhaust the pool and cause other users' requests to be preempted or dropped.

llama.cpp exits with an error if the model plus KV cache does not fit in available memory. vLLM preempts in-flight sequences when memory pressure is critical, which silently truncates long-running requests. Monitor memory metrics in production.
Prompt injection at the API layer:
the inference engine itself has no prompt sanitization. Applications are responsible for filtering user-supplied content before it reaches the model.

Think of the inference engine as the operating system kernel for LLM inference. The model weights are the application binary. The KV cache is working memory — it grows with each token generated and shrinks when a sequence ends. The scheduler is the process scheduler, deciding which requests get GPU time. PagedAttention is the virtual memory system, hiding memory fragmentation from the application layer by presenting each sequence with a clean contiguous view of its KV cache even though the physical pages are scattered throughout VRAM. The GPU is the CPU. The OpenAI-compatible API is the system call interface — your application does not need to know what hardware is underneath as long as the API contract is honored.

With llama.cpp, the analogy extends to embedded systems: the engine is compiled once and runs anywhere, trading throughput for portability, the same way an embedded RTOS trades advanced memory management for minimal footprint and cross-architecture support.
vLLM's PagedAttention eliminates KV cache fragmentation by mapping logical sequence pages to non-contiguous physical memory blocks, the same way OS virtual memory works.
Continuous batching keeps the GPU active across concurrent requests by dynamically admitting new sequences mid-batch rather than waiting for the current batch to complete.
llama.cpp's GGUF format encodes quantized weights, tokenizer, and metadata in a single portable file that loads on any hardware without Python or CUDA.
Both engines expose an OpenAI-compatible /v1/chat/completions API, making them drop-in replacements for client code written against the OpenAI SDK.
Select vLLM for GPU servers with concurrent users; select llama.cpp for CPU, Apple Silicon, edge, and embedded deployments.
Tensor parallelism in vLLM allows models larger than any single GPU's VRAM to be served by splitting layers across multiple GPUs.
Always validate quantized model accuracy on your specific task before deploying — quality degradation is small for Q4_K_M on most tasks but measurable for structured output and tool calling.
